nativeRange.habitat: Cryptocoryne lucens is found along the shaded margins of slow-moving streams and freshwater bodies in Sri Lanka, often growing in shallow water beneath rainforest canopy. In its natural habitat it commonly experiences seasonal water level changes that leave plants temporarily exposed to air (emersed) before being submerged again.

decorPreferences: plant in small groups around rocks or driftwood

avoidWith: Silver Dollars and other plant-eating fish

summary: Cryptocoryne Lucens is a compact, narrow-leaved crypt native to Sri Lanka, valued for its tolerance of low light and ease of care. Slow-growing but generally hardy once established, it is well suited to foreground or midground placement, though new plantings often shed leaves temporarily in a normal acclimation response known as crypt melt.
careNotes: Cryptocoryne Lucens is a small rosette plant — commonly called a crypt — native to streams in Sri Lanka, and generally considered one of the more compact members of the genus. It is well suited to foreground or midground placement in the aquarium. The leaves are narrow and lance-shaped, ranging from bright green to olive-green depending on light intensity. The species spreads by runners (stolons, horizontal stems that produce new plantlets), and once established can form a loose, bushy carpet, though growth is slow; planting multiple specimens close together helps fill in a foreground area more quickly. Lighting tolerance is broad — it has been reported to thrive under low light and also accepts moderate lighting. CO2 supplementation is not required, but the plant tends to respond with faster growth and richer color when it is provided. As with all crypts, it is a heavy root feeder — plant it in nutrient-rich aquarium substrate and supplement with root tabs near the root zone for best results. When planting, bury the roots up to (but NOT including) the crown where the leaves emerge from the rhizome; burying the crown causes rot. CRYPT MELT WARNING: like other Cryptocoryne species, C. lucens is well known for crypt melt — after planting, water-change shocks, or major parameter shifts, the plant will frequently shed most or all of its leaves over a few days. This is a normal acclimation response and the rhizome (the thick horizontal stem beneath the substrate) should be left in place; the plant generally regrows new leaves adapted to the new conditions within 2–4 weeks. Stable water parameters are the most reliable way to minimize melt.
breedingNotes: Propagated primarily by runners (stolons) that produce daughter plants alongside the parent; once a daughter plant has developed several leaves and its own root system it can be carefully separated from the runner and replanted elsewhere. Rhizome division is also possible: cut a healthy rhizome into sections, each retaining leaves and roots, and replant. Like other crypts, Cryptocoryne lucens can flower (producing the spathe-and-spadix inflorescence typical of Araceae) when grown emersed, but seed propagation is not practical in the home aquarium.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Confirmed Sri Lankan origin, small size (~2 inches in the aquarium), foreground placement, moderate lighting tolerance, temperature 68-82 °F (~20-28 °C), easy/adaptable care level, and confusion with Cryptocoryne parva.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-checked slow growth, low-light tolerance, ~3 inches tall at maturity, foreground-to-midground placement, runner-based spreading habit, and dense narrow bright-green-leaved carpeting behavior. Disagreed with Modern Aquarium on lighting (low vs moderate); both reads are reasonable and we set the typed lighting field to 'low' per genus consensus.
